New Zealand is a diverse country,

two long skinny islands where we mostly live

competitive pricing can be quite challenging,

It's quite common for a larger business to absorb

With a smaller business, they don't have that ability,

Well, um, my name's Edward Miller.
I work for an organization called Sitar,
where I undertake corporate research on a number
of different markets in a number
of different countries all around the world.
Um, thanks very much for the opportunity to submit.
Um, and thanks to the committee for all the work
that's been done in this space so far.
It's really great to see positive reform in this sector.
Um, like the Commerce Act, I was born in 1986
and like the Commerce Act,
I'm losing hair in all the wrong places.
And I think it's great to see, um, this initiative to try
and reform this legislation.
I mean, unfortunately here in New Zealand, we, we kind
of live now under the yoke
of oligopoly power in many places.
You know, we have non-competitive markets in, in banking,
energy, grocery building materials, telecoms, insurance,
a big chunk of workers
and consumers weekly wage bill goes into the oligopoly
sectors of the economy.
Um, in my prior role
as the researcher at Workers First Union,
we represent workers in a number of these sectors
where they were, um, unfortunately on the receiving end
of both, um, as consumers having to deal
with non-competitive markets, um, for, um, fund, you know,
getting access to basic goods and services,
but also as workers being in non-competitive labor markets
where there were very few employers,
very little competition.
Um, whether you like unions
or not, having a short, a small number
of employers in the market,
in the labor market means there's very little competition
and there's very few protection measures for workers.
So they end up being pushed towards unions
more than they otherwise would.
Um, we got here under the current act, we got
to this position under the current act, which was kind
of a product of that Chicago School of Regulation
of competition, regula regulation,
which put efficiency gains ahead of competition
for consumers.
So regardless of
how those efficiency gains were distributed,
whether they went to workers, consumers,
or whether they went to the, um, holders
of businesses themselves passed on as
as dividends to shareholders.
And unfortunately, that is the situation that has happened,
and it continues to happen a lot of the time.
I think that view is changing,
and I think the reforms in this legislation are really
helpful in getting us towards that.
But there are a few things in the rules that, um,
will keep us in that efficiency model,
and I'll talk to those at the end.
So I'm really glad to see the, um, targeting the problem
of creeping acquisitions.
I saw this up close in a number
of sectors in my previous work,
particularly in the waste sector in which essentially there
are now two companies that, um, run the vast majority
of waste concessions around the country and environ waste
and waste management, and also in the bus sector,
which looks like there are a number of companies,
but in fact there are essentially two Kinetic,
which owns Go Bus and NZ Bus
and Richie's, which is owned by KKR, the big, uh,
New York investment firm based outta the Cayman Islands.
Um, since then I've looked at another, a number
of other sectors like early childhood education,
aged care diagnostic services, medical imaging services,
primary care, dental care, all
of these quite fundamental support services, uh,
fundamental services, often services that are funded
by the public purse in which this problem of,
of creep creeping acquisitions
by private equity funds has meant there is very little
competition in the marketplace,
and often tax dollars are being plunged into paying
for services that are more expensive than they would
otherwise be because of those creeping acquisitions.
So I want to applaud the work that's been done
so far in this space.
I think the three year aggregation rule is a really
fantastic innovation, um,
and will help slow the consolidation in some
of those sectors and possibly create space
for new market players.
Um, there will always be a behavioral response
by firms to new rules.
And, um, some of those,
I'm sure private equity funds will start changing, um,
exactly what their acquisition structures look like
and how they do acquisitions when they do them timing wise.
So there might be an argument
to shift from a three year rule
to a five year rule in the future,
which probably aligns more tightly with
what a private equity fund strategy would look like.
Um, but there's behavioral changes either way as well.
The other behavioral response that you, we are likely to see
and we've seen in Australia is that when the rules come in,
then um, a whole lot
of acquisitions are brought forward in terms
of their existing timelines.
So you probably end up with some poor decision making
or poor deal making in the short term.
Might not be a huge amount that we can do about that.
Uh, but there will be behavioral responses.
Um, this, the new 47 E
and 47 F powers look to be really strong, innovative, um,
new rules that will make it easier for the commission
to in investigate those non notified transactions.
Um, hopefully this makes it a lot harder for mergers.
Um, that's substantially less in competition
to fly under the radar, uh,
but there is still a discovery gap
and the commission will will be at a material disadvantage
in terms of, uh, finding out about
acquisitions that are taking place.
Um, the comparison here to Australia is important.
They've moved to this mandatory notification regime
with full suspensory powers.
Um, the Australian view,
or maybe the ac CC view was
that the voluntary regime is essentially asking a shark
to report itself to a lifeguard.
Um, but once a shark has taken a chunk out of a swim,
it's very hard to stitch that back on.
So sewing things back together is a lot more difficult.
And having those mandatory powers to suspend, um,
or to suspend all
of those non notified transactions would give the commission
a bit more of an opportunity to deal
with the material disadvantages that they face in terms
of being able to engage with all of the acquisitions
that are taking place or all of the mergers.
Um, so, you know, it's a design question more than anything,
and I think it would be interesting
to get more feedback from the ACCC on
how the implementation is, is looking there.
Uh, the predatory pricing changes I think
are really fantastic.
Um, and I wanna applaud the work
that's being done in this space, moving, um, from
that purpose test, which is next to impossible to prove, uh,
purpose on the part of, um, predatory pricing
to a more objective test is a really important one.
And it's a space where we are going a bit further
than Australia as well.
So it's great to see that, you know, we, we always seem
to have, well, uh, for good reason, we always look
to Australia, uh, that, that good reason being
because so much of our economy is owned
by Australian businesses, um, not just by the big monopoly
operators, but also by, um,
Australian domicile private equity funds.
So looking to Australia is important,
but knowing when to go further than Australia
is also really valuable.
Um, and that's where I wanted to just wrap up
by saying there are a couple of areas
where I think this legislation could have gone further,
and it would be great to go further in this respect.
I think, you know, it's a bit
of a missed opportunity in some respects,
because this is, I guess the, the
what the second major reform of the Commerce Act
that we've had since it's inception.
Um, we are still stuck in the,
I guess, efficiency remains the underlying, uh,
legislative impulse within, within the proposed changes.
And the, the evidence of that is
that section 67 is still there as a kind of gateway test.
You know, the efficiency goes in the
competition machine if you like.
Uh, that's been left as is.
So there are, the way the law operates, you have two, um,
potential gateways.
There's substantial lessening in competition,
but sub, uh, mergers
or acquisitions that substantially lessen competition can
still proceed if they are deemed to, um,
satisfy the efficiency defense if they result in
public benefits.
And those public benefits do not need to.
Um, there doesn't need to be any evidence
of those public benefits really accrue to members
of the public necessarily.
So we can unfortunately,
under six section 67 approve mergers
that increase total welfare, um,
or have efficiency gains regardless of
where those efficiency gains end up,
whether they're in the pockets of workers and consumers,
or whether they're in the pockets of, uh,
shareholders from the offshore owned firms that run much
of the majority the monopoly economy.
So I think that's a major emission from the,
the, um, reforms.
And I think the second emission is
that we don't have anything in this, these changes
that will allow us to roll back the three
or four decades of consolidation that we have had.
We don't have new powers for the commission
or for other, uh, regulators to be able to break up some
of the oligopoly markets that we have
and take us back to an era of greater competition.
And I think that's the important discussion moving forward.
How do we roll back the failures of the past regime
to move forward and make sure that all the great changes
that are coming in, uh, that I've talked about, um,
have the teeth to be able to benefit workers

very quick question, Dr.

Uh, good morning committee. Thank

My name is Bianca Bebe.
I'm a public health researcher
and chair of, uh, fired up stilettos, which advocates
for the labor rights of independent contractors doing
various kinds of adult entertainment
and sex work in managed venues throughout New Zealand.
Um, so when fired up stilettos first came
to Parliament in 2023, um, our petition asked for,
among other things, the right
to collectively bargain while maintaining our
independent contracting status.
Um, and in his official response to US Ministers Simpson,
uh, did not commit the government
to freestanding legislation,
but said that he would consider us in changes
to the Commerce Act and wanted to thank the government
and Minister Simpson in particular,
because that has clearly happened.
Um, the changes that we see
to the Commerce Act will indeed allow us to go on the path
to collectively bargaining while staying
independent contractors.
Um, but we have a few, um, suggestions
that can give greater effect
to the government's aims without risking,
um, cartel behavior.
So clearly we strongly support the government's plan
to implement a notification regime under Schedule eight.
What we need is clarity on who can notify.
Um, when we called the Commerce Commission, they said
that in this legislation,
it's unclear if each dancer at a venue would have
to individually notify the commission,
or if contractors could sign up with a legal entity
who can then bargain on their behalf.
Uh, we wanna start using this legislation
as soon as it's enacted.
So ideally, as few bureaucratic roadblocks
as possible would be great.
The ideal scenario would be for independent contractors
to have the freedom to sign up with a legal entity, charity,
or union, who can then notify the commission
and bargain on behalf of its members.
Um, the streamlined process would reduce bureaucratic
paperwork for the commission
and encourage greater uptake among contractors,
which will intend in turn, improve competition
and working conditions in our sector.
Uh, secondly, we'd like to discuss application fees
to notify or be granted a class exemption.
We know that application fees are not generally set in
statutory law, but fees have the power to make
or break this legislation.
Uh, the best legislation in the world is not useful if the
price to access it is out of reach
of those who actually need it.
Um, we note that the legislation gives the commerce
commission the discretion to waive, um,
or reduce fees if they would present a quote undue hardship.
Um, but this solution would likely present even more
problems because the things that can lead
to undue hardship in our industry are frequently
inappropriate to discuss with people
who are untrained in matters of sexual harm.
So it would instead make much more sense for there
to be no application fee
for anyone applying under Schedule eight, since we are,
by definition, relatively low revenue contractors
who do not pose a cartel risk
through bargaining collectively.
And finally, I wanna talk about retaliation
and victimization, which is the most important aspect.
I cannot possibly overstate the level of impunity
with which venues have grown accustomed to operating.
When dancers have tried to negotiate with clubs
to remove even the most egregious
and exploitative clauses from their contracts, clubs, love
to just smirk in our faces
and say, why would we talk to you girls?
Turn 18 every day.
Multiple dancers have been explicitly told
by venue management
that they would be fired if they even followed,
fired up stilettos on their Instagram accounts,
or liked one of our posts,
particularly in places like Auckland
and Wellington with a high student population combined
with a high cost of living
and a dearth of jobs that accommodate a student's schedule.
Clubs have evolved an entire business model
around creating extractive predatory conditions
for contracted workers
and immediately firing them if they object,
because they know there's always a new
teenager who needs to pay rent.
If there is no obligation for these venues
to act in good faith, they will not.
If this legislation were to pass in its current form,
independent contractors would be able
to notify the Commerce Commission of their intent
to collectively bargain, and they would be immediately
fired for doing so.
That means no one would actually use the law as intended,
and the government's hard work would be wasted.
We don't want that to happen. So we need the protections in
place that will allow us to use this law.
I wanna end by clarifying
what our suggested changes will not do.
They will not cause any conflict
with the newly introduced gateway test
of the Employment Relations Act or
otherwise interfere with the government's aim
to establish greater clarity on the difference
between employees and contractors.
Fired up stilettos has been consistent in saying
that we were hired as contractors
and we would like to be treated as contractors.
When we contractors are allowed
to collectively negotiate our contracts,
we will increase market competition
and reduce the incentive for businesses
to misclassify their workers in the first place.
Our suggested changes will work in tandem
with many other initiatives the government is currently
working on rather than against 'em.
So to conclude, this notification regime is a great
start, but it needs teeth.
We encourage you to, uh, ensure the changes you're working
so hard to achieve cannot simply be ignored
by bad faith actors exploiting their market dominance
to reduce competition, increase prices for consumers,
and worsen working conditions for contractors.
So let's make sure the law applies equally
to everyone. Thank you.

you talk about, um, the, the screen, uh,
industry Workers Act of 22.

So for instance, um, section 24
of siwa prohibits retaliatory termination

And Section 26 requires all parties

That is similar to what is currently in the proposed changes

However, 90, or sorry 97 A and B,
but 97 a specifies

And that is exactly the bind

Right? We are not trying to be reclassified as employees,
we want to be treated as contractors.
Um, but our independence

And indeed, our, uh, rights as people has not either.

section, um, in, uh, CIWA, which obviously is

Um, we recognize that not all

There's just those two sections.

that are un uh, unable to come to a consensus
or face some kind of a dispute in their bargaining process,
there needs to be some kind of third party that's able
to break through when there is some kind of gridlock.
Um, the Commerce Commission, uh, in our initial hearings,
um, acknowledge that many
of the terms in our contracts are likely anti-competitive.
Um, and they can see how we would have a substantial case,
but we simply do not have the market power for them
to take the time to go through a court process
for what we need.
So they have said they're already overloaded
and they can't help us.
So what Ciwa did as a workaround of that on, uh, section 72,
it said that parties that are in a dispute can apply
to the ERA for determination of the dispute.
Um, and that should be resolved, uh, uh, uh, in accordance
with how the ERA would normally do between, um,
bargaining employers
and employees in that very limited context.
Not in order to reclassify or misclassify people,
but simply to have a third party that is equipped for
that specific dynamic, um,
which can be applied appropriately even while maintaining

clauses in their contracts as we do.

basically punitive fines that are in contracts.

Um, then also many clubs implement

I included in our written submission a picture of, uh,
the mermaids contract,

So it's, uh, a quite an irony that we find ourselves in,

But what has happened is, uh,
strip clubs have engaged in explicitly anti-competitive

and, uh, concentrated the market to the extent

and, uh, egregiously violated, um, uh,
workplace relations and safety.
Thank you so much. Um, thank you so much, Bianca,

thank you for answering those questions.

Firstly, we do acknowledge the practical reality
that New Zealanders have been struggling with cost
of living pressures and
that getting the competition law settings right has an
important role to play in both delivering competitive
markets for the benefit of consumers
and also addressing cost of living pressures,
which many New Zealanders still face today.
However, in our opinion, the proposed introduction
of a bright line prohibition on
below cost pricing does not address these pressures,
and in fact, is likely to have the opposite effect
of higher prices for consumers.
Firstly, we are concerned that the proposed changes
to predatory pricing were not included in the comprehensive
review or consultation that was undertaken
by MDM to the Commerce Act.
There has been no evidence provided,
and we are not aware of any evidence
that predatory pricing is a widespread
or practical concern in New Zealand.
To the extent that there are isolated examples
of predatory pricing, we consider that the current law,
which was only recently amended in 2023, is robust enough
to address anti-competitive below cost pricing.
So there is no evidential basis to suggest
that a change is warranted in addition to there not being
evidence that changes required.
We have three main issues
with the proposed new Bright Line prohibition.
First bright line prohibitions are only appropriate
where the relevant conduct is almost always harmful.
With respect to below cost pricing, it is clear
that consumers benefit from this in the short run
and in most cases benefit from such low
prices in the long run.
There are likely to be very few cases where
below cost pricing is harmful.
The economics literature is clear
that predatory pricing is a very expensive way
to deter competition and has a very low success rate.
This is why it is important
that an actual impact on competition of the
below cost pricing is undertaken
as is required by the current law.
The second concern we have with the new, uh, prohibition is,
although there is an exception, that those exceptions
for promotional pricing do not apply,
where there's a pattern of behavior over a sustained period,
now that carve out is a likely to prohibit routine behavior
that consumers benefit from including where, um,
businesses sell perishable
or short dated stock below cost
to encourage consumption rather than wastage
selling last year's or last season's models
or stock at below cost to make room for new models
selling goods below cost to charities
or social groups to support charitable initiatives
and employee discount schemes or arrangements.
Third, we are concerned that the proposed introduction
of this, um, deeming provision will have a chilling
effect in our experience.
Businesses take their competition
or obligations very seriously.
The proposed prohibition relies on economic concepts
that are susceptible to different interpretations
and are not standard everyday accounting
or financial concepts that businesses use
to make everyday decisions.
Faced with the new Bright Line test
businesses will err on the side of compliance,
likely leading to higher prices across the economy.
We do not consider that this is in the best interest
of consumers or is consistent
with the government's objective of going for growth.
Our strong recommendation,
therefore would be to delete clause six from the bill.
Now pass on to Troy.
Thank you Brad, and good morning to the committee.
I just wanna thank the committee
for their work on this bill.
We all recognize how important it is for New Zealand
to get the competition or settings correct.
And also just want to emphasize there are aspects
of the bill we support some of the changes that are proposed
to make it easier for businesses to collaborate in relation
to positive initiatives such as collaboration,
sustainability, supply chain initiatives, health and safety.
We really support the current authorization regime
with the commission as cumbersome,
burdensome, lengthy, costly.
We just don't see it achieving
what it needs to in those spaces.
And we've certainly seen positive initiatives fall
by the wayside because of that.
So I encourage that. What I wanna focus on in my couple
of minutes today is just the proposed change
to the substantial lessening of competition standard.
In particular, the proposal that
that gets changed across the entirety of the comac,
including in relation to the behavioral
aspects of the Commiss Act.
I am very concerned about a proposal
that would make New Zealand bespoke
and different to Australia.
The New Zealand market is fundamentally integrated
with the Australian market.
There's a whole lot of benefits
to New Zealanders from having a integrated, uh,
closely aligned regulatory framework
across Australia and New Zealand.
A number of businesses operate across both those.
They set their compliance regimes, their standards,
et cetera, to reflect both those jurisdictions operations.
There was a proposal in the recent amendments in Australia
to change the substantial lesson
of competition standard across the entirety
of Australia's competition
or statute, including in relation
to this behavioral, uh, prohibitions.
But ultimately that did not occur
and that did not occur for good reason.
So we are concerned that we're looking to go alone
and different from Australia.
We do consider
that chip making this change in New Zealand will actually
reduce clarity for New Zealand businesses
or reduce efficiency for New Zealand businesses.
And also there's just no need for the change.
So the bill says that the change is for the avoidance
of doubt, and I assume it says that
because basically what the proposal is, is changing it
to make it reflect case law.
But when Parliament makes a change to a statute,
the courts assume that parliament must have intended
something to be different.
So if this change is made, the judges,
the courts will assume that this is supposed
to lower the threshold for when a, uh, substantial leasing
of competition can occur.
And we are concerned that that will be interpreted, uh,
to capture, uh,
larger businesses doing things which are actually ultimately
positive, uh, for customers such as organic growth
or successful innovation,
which obviously naturally might strengthen a business's
existing position, but it's not something
that should be deterred or prevented by competition law.
So, for example, a large business seeking to, uh, innovate
and get a patient across a new design
or a new technology, uh,
or overseas businesses that have got new innovations,
will they be concerned about launching
that in New Zealand if they could be deemed
to entrench their current position in a market?
So our view is
that risks taking New Zealand on a divergent path from
Australia and at risks, undermining growth
and innovation in New Zealand.
And also, I know there's often a temptation
to say when there's a law chain, well,
of course the Commerce Commission wouldn't enforce it in
areas where it shouldn't.
But it's important that the committee's aware that
of course the Commerce Act can also be enforced
by third parties, uh, taking, uh, private actions.
And so a concern around unfounded
or spurious claims being taken,
if someone does do something which a competitor thinks
actually makes it, makes them larger
or could somehow be deemed to
entrench their position even though it is a positive
innovation or a new technology.
So we submit that the substantial less in competition test
should remain as it currently is
and reliant on the case law
interpretation as it currently stands.
Thank you, Troy. And, and look, I'm conscious of time
and we are keen to leave, um, a couple of minutes
for questions, but maybe just briefly on the behavioral
remedies proposal, as I mentioned, we are, you know,
very supportive of that.
We think that it's actually going to, um, permit more m
and a activity in New Zealand transactions that may
otherwise not have occurred, um,
because of the, I guess the,
the constraints currently under the legislation to, uh,
for the commission to only accept structural remedies, um,
you know, has, has been a problem in our experience.
I think just one thing I would like to leave the committee
with based on some of the other submissions
that we've been hearing, we just wanna be really clear that,
um, you know, that the,
the commission would still ultimately need to be satisfied
that the merger, including any remedy,
would actually not have an effect
or likely effect on competition
or substantially lessening competition.
So, so it's not about relaxing, um,
the fundamental prohibition against
anti-competitive mergers.
It's, it's not about that at all.
Um, the commission, if it's not convinced
that potential competition harm can be sufficiently removed
to resolve the case, it actually
doesn't need to accept the remedy.
So, um, we do, uh, you know, we're really supportive
of the proposal subject to our, uh, written submissions,
um, proposed changes.
Thank you. Thank you so much for your submission.
We don't have any time left for Ians. Thank you.
Um, next we have a
deport again on Zoom.
Sorry about that. Hang on. What's going on here?
Hang on, we can hear you. Debbie can't. Yeah, hang on.
Can't see you though. Yes, we can as well.
Now, thank you so much
for your submission and thank you for your time.
We have five minutes with you.
Okay. So basically, um, I worked in the supermarket
and, uh, energy sector as a teenager.
That's where I worked in the supermarket.
And when I worked in the supermarket,
there was something circulating that the company
that I worked for wanted
to be the only supermarket in the country.
That's actually quite, and they actually gave it a good
crack until it was lit too late
because the Commerce Commission sat back and it happened.
It's actually happening now in the energy sector.
How is it that, uh, Genesis Energy were allowed
to buy Energy online, changed the name to Frank Energy,
and then absorb it last year?
How is it okay that a few years ago,
m Nuclear Energy were allowed to buy trust
power and absorb it?
That is anti-competitive behavior.
The Commerce Commission are actually part of the problem.
Who's regulating the Commerce Commission?
You give them all these powers
and they complain
to them about breaches of the Fair Trading Act.
For example, two Degrees
started slapping a fair use policy on all their plans.
And, um, I became aware of this when somebody, uh,
posted a screenshot on Spark's Facebook page of the, the,
um, of what two degrees were doing, and it's hidden cap.
You cannot slap a fair use policy on a, um, on a, uh,
unlimited slash endless plan because it's a hidden cap
and the terms, and Condit says, oh,
we will terminate your services without notice.
Um, hold up. And the hidden cap was actually 40 gig.
I'm sorry, but the Commerce Commissioner actually part
of the problem, who, who knows, who remembers, um,
simply squeezed that used to be a branded drink here.
Frugal, bought them in 2009
and asked them late years later, I believe they lied
to the Commerce Commission, the Commerce Commission
that Frugal Buy Simply Squeezed,
which would've been around about 30 years.
So they Commerce Commissioner actually part of the problem.
What are we doing about the Commerce Commission?
'cause you raise a complaint and they don't wanna know.
So my suggestion is we need
to regulate the Commerce Commission.
We need to oversee the Commerce Commission to make sure
that they're actually complying with, um, the law itself.
'cause they, they, they don't take complaints seriously.
I raised alarms with them and they, they, so no interest.
Thank you, Debbie. Um, are there any questions, uh,
members for Debbie?
Uh, no. Debbie, there are no questions.
Thank you so much for your contribution.
Okay, thank you. Thank
You. Bye bye-Bye.
Uh, so now we move into private session.
